Iran blames the US for stalled peace talks, citing a naval blockade as a key obstacle, while Iran's IRGC captured two foreign vessels and fired on a third in the Strait of Hormuz. The US Senate rejected a measure to curb Trump's authority to wage war on Iran, and the Pentagon chief fired Navy Secretary John Phelan.

Will another film star be able to sway the election in India’s Tamil Nadu? | Elections News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-23T07:42:26Z

Tamil Nadu votes to elect its state assembly, with actor-turned-politician C Joseph Vijay entering the race and turning a traditionally two-party contest into a three-way fight. Vijay's party challenges the long-dominant DMK and the main opposition AIADMK, which is allied with the BJP. The state has a history of electing film stars as leaders.

Israeli strike kills five in Gaza, including three children | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-23T07:53:15Z

The EU's energy commissioner warned that the energy crisis could impact prices for months or years.

UK bill bans anyone born after 2008 from ever buying tobacco | Health News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-22T10:48:13Z

The UK Parliament has approved a bill that will permanently ban anyone born on or after January 1, 2009, from ever buying tobacco. The law, described as a historic public health intervention, aims to create a 'smoke-free generation' and includes new powers to regulate tobacco and vaping products. It will also expand smoke-free zones to include playgrounds and outside schools.

Myanmar’s military government rebuffed on peace talks offer | Politics News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T21:21:37Z

Myanmar's military-backed government, led by President Min Aung Hlaing, has invited opposition armed groups to peace talks with a deadline of July 31. Key rebel groups, including the Karen National Union and the Chin National Front, swiftly rejected the offer, citing the 2021 coup and a desire for a federal democratic system free from military influence. The military government's recent election has been widely criticized as a sham, and its peace talks since 2022 have yielded little result.

Peru’s election chief steps down amid frustration over long vote count | Elections News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T22:14:40Z

The head of Peru's election authority, Piero Corvetto, has resigned amid widespread anger over logistical chaos and delays in counting votes from the April 12 general election. He denied irregularities but stepped down to boost public confidence ahead of an anticipated June 7 presidential runoff. Right-wing candidate Keiko Fujimori leads the first round, but her opponent remains unclear as vote counting continues, with final results expected by May 15.

Trump administration sues Southern Poverty Law Center on fraud charges | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T23:08:16Z

The U.S. Department of Justice has indicted the Southern Poverty Law Center (SPLC) on federal fraud charges, alleging it improperly raised millions from donors to pay informants within far-right groups like the Ku Klux Klan. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche accused the civil rights group of funding the very ideology it claims to fight. The SPLC, which says the program was used to monitor threats and share information with law enforcement, faces charges including wire fraud and conspiracy to commit money laundering.

Read original article

Paraguay plans to accept 25 third-country migrant deportees from US | Migration News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T23:10:21Z

Paraguay has announced it will accept an initial group of 25 non-citizen deportees from the United States as part of President Donald Trump's mass deportation push. The country stated each case was evaluated individually, respecting national sovereignty and international law. This makes Paraguay one of several countries participating in 'third-country' deportation agreements with the U.S., a program criticized by advocacy groups over human rights concerns and the stability of some destination countries.

Tensions persist over Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz and a US naval blockade.

Zarbiv has publicly boasted about the destruction in Gaza and says he represents fellow soldiers.

EU court rules Hungary’s LGBTQ law violates human rights | LGBTQ News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T16:36:44Z

The European Court of Justice ruled that Hungary's 2021 anti-LGBTQ legislation breaches EU law on multiple levels. The law, which banned the 'promotion of homosexuality' to minors, was criticized for stigmatizing LGBTQ people. The ruling comes after nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who introduced the law, was recently defeated in elections.

Israeli sexual violence helping push Palestinians from West Bank: Report | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T16:38:13Z

A report by the West Bank Protection Consortium details at least 16 cases of conflict-related sexual violence by Israeli settlers and soldiers, contributing to Palestinian displacement. Researchers found threats to women and children were a decisive reason for families leaving their homes. The report states Israeli soldiers present during incidents did not prevent or properly investigate the attacks.

Flu vaccine no longer mandatory for soldiers, says US military chief | Military News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T18:04:31Z

US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced the flu vaccine will no longer be mandatory for military personnel, citing principles of 'medical autonomy' and religious freedom. He criticized the mandate as 'overreaching' and 'not rational.' The decision is part of a broader shift in vaccine policy under the Trump administration, which has rolled back several public health mandates.

Ukraine says Druzhba pipeline running Russian oil to Europe can resume work | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T18:27:14Z

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy stated the Druzhba pipeline, which carries Russian oil to Europe, is ready to resume operations after repairs. He hopes this will lead to the release of a blocked EU loan, which Hungary had vetoed. EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said she expects a 'positive decision' on the loan soon, following the pipeline's reopening and Hungary's recent election results.

US forces detain Iran-linked tanker Tifani with ceasefire talks on edge | News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T18:45:32Z

US forces detained the sanctioned oil tanker M/T Tifani in the Indian Ocean, accusing it of smuggling Iranian crude. The Pentagon stated the action was to disrupt illicit networks supporting Iran. The detention occurred as a US-Iran ceasefire was about to expire and talks were uncertain.

Russian police raid book publisher accused of pushing ‘gay propaganda’ | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T19:05:41Z

Russian police raided the publishing house Eksmo, seizing books and questioning its CEO on suspicion of disseminating 'homosexual propaganda.' The raid is part of a broader crackdown on LGBTQ expression under laws banning the promotion of same-sex relations. Russia's ultraconservative social turn has intensified since its invasion of Ukraine.

Two CIA agents reportedly killed in car crash in Mexican state of Chihuahua | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T19:49:14Z

Two agents reportedly from the CIA were killed in a car crash in Mexico's Chihuahua state. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum announced an investigation to determine if their presence violated Mexican law, which requires federal authorization for foreign agents. She reiterated that joint US-Mexico ground operations are not permitted, despite US threats of military action against cartels.

Trump’s US Fed nominee Warsh vows independence, says he’s no ‘sock puppet’ | Banks News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:05:52Z

Federal Reserve nominee Kevin Warsh addressed concerns about his independence from President Trump during a Senate confirmation hearing. He pushed back against claims he would be a 'sock puppet,' emphasizing the importance of monetary policy independence. Warsh called for reforms at the Fed, including a new approach to inflation and changes to its communication practices.

Trump announces Iran ceasefire extension but says blockade remains | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:22:17Z

President Trump announced an extension of the ceasefire with Iran, citing a request from Pakistani mediators. He stated the US military will hold off attacks to allow Iran time to submit a unified proposal to end the war, but the naval blockade will continue. This followed earlier statements where Trump opposed an extension, and Iranian officials condemned the blockade as an 'act of war.'

Sudan refugees returning home face new ‘struggle for survival’: UN | Sudan war News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:32:32Z

Nearly 4 million people have returned to their places of origin in Sudan, but face a new 'struggle for survival' due to destroyed infrastructure and lack of essential services. The UN's International Organisation for Migration warned that returns are unsustainable without urgent investment. The ongoing war has killed tens of thousands and displaced millions since April 2023.

EU eyes options as Iran conflict threatens jet fuel shortages | European Union News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:33:13Z

The EU is considering jet fuel imports from the US and new reserve quotas as the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz threatens supplies. An EU transport commissioner said there is no evidence of 'actual shortages' currently and widespread flight cancellations are not expected. The bloc is establishing a 'fuel observatory' to monitor the situation and may propose minimum stock obligations.

US issues more Iran sanctions on eve of possible talks in Pakistan | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:42:21Z

The US issued new sanctions targeting 14 individuals and entities accused of helping Iran acquire weapon components. The Treasury Department stated the move is part of increasing financial pressure on Iran. The sanctions were announced as talks between the US and Iran, scheduled in Pakistan, remain uncertain due to the ongoing US blockade.

Nearly 8,000 people died or disappeared on migration routes in 2025: IOM | News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:43:11Z

The UN's International Organization for Migration reported nearly 8,000 people died or disappeared on migration routes in 2025, with sea routes to Europe being the most deadly. The figure represents a drop from 2024, partly due to unverified cases. The IOM stressed that migration routes are shifting and remain dangerous, with over 82,000 deaths recorded since 2014.

Pro-Palestine legal aid requests stay high in 2025 amid US campus pressure | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T20:48:47Z

Legal aid group Palestine Legal received 1,131 requests for support related to pro-Palestine advocacy in 2025, a figure that remains high despite a drop from 2024. Technofacism? Why Palantir’s pro-West ‘manifesto’ has critics alarmed | Technology News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T06:54:57Z

Palantir Technologies has posted a summary of a book by its CEO and head of corporate affairs, which critics have labeled a 'manifesto' promoting 'technofascism.' The document calls for national service, a moral duty for tech companies to participate in defense, and embraces religion in public life. Critics argue these positions, advocating for hard power and cultural hierarchies, represent a dangerous ideology from a company whose revenue depends on defense and intelligence contracts.

A two-week ceasefire is set to expire, with the US maintaining a blockade of Iranian ports and Iran demanding its lifting before talks. Meanwhile, Israeli strikes continue in southern Lebanon, and various international actors, including Russia and China, have expressed concerns or called for an extension of the ceasefire.

Mughal-era pigeon training survives in heart of India’s capital | Arts and Culture | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T07:24:06Z

In Old Delhi, practitioners continue the ancient Mughal tradition of pigeon-rearing, known as kabootarbaazi. Keepers train birds to fly in precise formations and navigate long distances, a skill passed down through generations. For many, the rooftop gatherings are a therapeutic escape from the city's chaos, fostering community and peace.

Virginia redistricting vote: What polls suggest and what voters will decide | US Midterm Elections 2026 News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T07:24:23Z

Voters in Virginia are deciding on a measure to redraw the state's congressional map, which could shift several seats in favor of Democrats. The proposal would create eight safely Democratic districts and two that lean Democratic, potentially helping the party gain control of the US House. A recent poll shows a small lead for supporters of the amendment.

Tennis duo Sabalenka and Alcaraz sweep top Laureus sports honours | Tennis News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T07:44:00Z

Aryna Sabalenka and Carlos Alcaraz were crowned Sportswoman and Sportsman of the Year at the Laureus Awards after finishing 2025 as the top-ranked players. Alcaraz won two Grand Slams, while Sabalenka won the US Open and reached two other major finals. Other winners included Barcelona's Lamine Yamal as Young Sportsperson and Paris Saint-Germain as World Team of the Year.

My dreams in Iran were already dead before the ceasefire came | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T08:21:40Z

A 28-year-old video editing assistant in Tehran describes how the war has wrecked his life, even though he was not physically injured. He lost his job and cannot find new work that pays a livable wage, forcing him to consider returning to his hometown where opportunities are bleak. The personal account details the economic and psychological damage inflicted by the conflict.

As Iran crisis drags on, fears of global food catastrophe grow | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T09:02:55Z

Analysts warn that the full impact of the Iran conflict on global food prices has yet to be felt, with a lag expected between rising input costs and higher shelf prices. The severity depends on how long shipping disruptions continue in the Strait of Hormuz. The FAO has warned of a potential global food 'catastrophe,' with poor people in Africa and Asia being the most affected.

Kenya’s John Korir, Sharon Lokedi win Boston Marathon on record-breaking day | Athletics News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T09:06:06Z

John Korir broke the Boston Marathon course record, winning in 2:01:52, the fifth-fastest marathon time ever. Sharon Lokedi defended her women's title, finishing in 2:18:51. Kenyan athletes dominated, taking the top four spots in the women's race, while American Jess McClain set a national record for an American woman at Boston.

Usain Bolt hopes Australian sprint sensation Gout has strong support system | Athletics | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T09:18:12Z

Usain Bolt has advised 18-year-old Australian sprint sensation Gout Gout to surround himself with a strong support team to stay focused on his career. Bolt warned that distractions can come with success and emphasized the importance of keeping track and field as the priority. Gout, who has drawn comparisons to Bolt, is set to make his Diamond League debut in June.

Spain, Slovenia, Ireland push EU to debate Israel pact suspension | Gaza News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T09:32:43Z

Spain, Slovenia, and Ireland have formally requested the EU debate suspending its association agreement with Israel, accusing it of violating human rights and international law. They cite the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, violence in the West Bank, and a proposed Israeli law on the death penalty for Palestinians. The ministers argue the EU can no longer remain 'on the sidelines.'

Clean electricity meets all new demand, curbing fossil fuels, says Ember | Energy News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T10:11:07Z

The energy think tank Ember reports that low-emissions sources met all new global electricity demand in 2025 for the first time, led by solar and wind power. This left no room for fossil fuel growth, marking what Ember calls a turning point. However, analysts note the system must still prove it can meet extreme peak demand, not just average growth.

CJ McCollum, Hawks rally late to shock Knicks in Game 2 of NBA playoffs | Basketball News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T03:49:42Z

The Atlanta Hawks staged a late comeback to defeat the New York Knicks 107-106 in Game 2 of their NBA playoff series, tying the series 1-1. Led by CJ McCollum's game-high 32 points, including key shots in the final minutes, the Hawks erased an eight-point deficit in the last five minutes. The Knicks had a chance to win at the buzzer, but Mikal Bridges missed a final shot.

Read original article

Algeria’s ex-minister of industry jailed in high-profile corruption case | Corruption News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T03:59:03Z

Former Algerian Industry Minister Ali Aoun has been sentenced to five years in prison after being convicted of corruption. The case, which involved the irregular sale of metal waste, also saw several other officials and businessmen receive prison sentences ranging from three to ten years. These convictions are part of an ongoing anti-corruption drive launched by President Abdelmadjid Tebboune.

Read original article

Japan lifts ban on lethal weapons exports in major shift of pacifist policy | Weapons News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T04:46:17Z

Japan's cabinet has lifted a long-standing ban on exporting lethal weapons, marking a major shift from its post-World War II pacifist policies. The change will allow Japan to sell weapons like fighter jets and missiles to eligible countries that commit to using them in accordance with the UN Charter. This policy shift follows a recent $7 billion deal with Australia for Japan to build warships.

Iran war forces job losses, reverse migration in India’s ceramic hub | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-21T06:05:36Z

The ceramics industry in Morbi, India, has been severely impacted by a fuel crisis stemming from the US-Israel war with Iran, leading to widespread factory shutdowns and job losses. This has forced migrant workers, who form a majority of the workforce, to return to their home states. The article profiles one such worker, Pradeep Kumar, who lost his job and returned to Uttar Pradesh with his family.

The Atlantic says it stands by its reporting and will defend against the lawsuit, which Patel calls 'replete with false and obviously fabricated allegations'.

Fears over Ethiopia peace deal as TPLF restores Tigray government | Conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-20T22:05:23Z

The Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) has announced it is taking back control of the Tigray regional government, effectively ending the 2022 Pretoria peace agreement with Ethiopia's federal government. The TPLF accuses the federal government of violating the agreement and provoking armed conflict. This move sparks fears of a resumption of the deadly war that killed hundreds of thousands and displaced millions between 2020 and 2022.

Apple CEO Tim Cook will step down on September 1, ending a nearly 15-year tenure during which the company's market value soared by more than $3.6 trillion. He will be succeeded by John Ternus, Apple's head of hardware engineering. Cook will remain with the company as executive chairman, assisting with aspects like engaging with global policymakers.

Around 45 others were injured, with Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi expressing condolences and announcing relief for the victims' families.

Government measures in Yemen have stabilized the Yemeni riyal's value but have created a severe liquidity crisis, with people facing unprecedented cash shortages. Banks and exchange firms are refusing or limiting conversions of foreign currency, paralyzing businesses and creating a black market. The economic meltdown stems from a decade-long war that has left both the government and Houthi rebels strapped for cash and struggling to provide basic services.

Police suspect the case involves the unlawful disposal of unclaimed corpses and have launched a forensic investigation, vowing to hold any responsible parties accountable.

This marks North Korea's seventh such launch this year and its fourth in April. The launches violate UN Security Council resolutions and come as China and the US prepare for a summit where North Korea is expected to be discussed.

The Norwegian Nobel Committee stated the prize is nontransferable, and Machado is coordinating her return to Venezuela with the US government.

He was joined by podcaster Joe Rogan, a proponent of ibogaine. The order directs at least $50 million to states advancing such programmes and comes as the FDA prepares to issue priority vouchers and clear the way for the first US human trials of ibogaine.

Ukrainian police stormed the building after failed negotiations and shot the attacker dead. The assailant was identified as a 58-year-old man born in Moscow, and at least 10 others were hospitalized.

However, Morocco's promised asylum law is not yet implemented, leaving refugees with limited state support and dependent on UNHCR registration.

This is part of President Claudia Sheinbaum's harder line on cartels, a shift from her predecessor's policy, amid pressure from US President Donald Trump to combat drug trafficking.

Sudan’s Prime Minister: This is the path out of the horrors of war | Sudan war | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T06:58:38Z

In an opinion piece, Sudan's Prime Minister Kamil Idris outlines his Hope Government's initiative for peace. The initiative calls for a comprehensive ceasefire under international supervision, the assembly of rebel militia in designated locations, and facilitating the return of displaced people. It also proposes a model of transitional justice and a political track aimed at reaching an elected government through Sudanese-Sudanese dialogue.

Palestinian Prisoner’s Day: What happened in Palestine on April 17, 1971? | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T06:59:48Z

The article marks Palestinian Prisoner's Day, commemorated on April 17 since 1974. It notes that nearly 10,000 Palestinians are currently held in Israeli prisons, with about a third held under administrative detention without charge or trial. The report highlights Israel's new death penalty law targeting Palestinians, which rights groups call discriminatory, and details the detention of hundreds of children in military courts.

Myanmar pardons over 4,000 prisoners, including deposed president | Prison News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T07:47:39Z

Myanmar's new president, coup leader Min Aung Hlaing, has granted amnesty to 4,335 prisoners. The pardon includes former President Win Myint and commutes all death sentences to life imprisonment. The article also notes that Aung San Suu Kyi's jail sentence has been reduced by one-sixth, according to her lawyer, though it is unclear if she will be moved to house arrest.

Read original article

Rapper d4vd arrested on suspicion of murdering 14-year-old girl | Crime News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T08:23:11Z

American rapper d4vd, whose real name is David Anthony Burke, has been arrested in Los Angeles on suspicion of murdering 14-year-old Celeste Rivas. Police found Rivas's dismembered body in the trunk of an impounded Tesla registered to Burke seven months ago. Burke's lawyers stated they will vigorously defend his innocence, and the DA's office will review the case for formal charges.

Read original article

Australia scrambles to secure energy as war on Iran fuels uncertainty | US-Israel war on Iran | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T10:09:09Z

Australia is facing fuel shortages due to the war on Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has disrupted 20% of global oil and LNG shipments. The government is responding with fuel tax cuts and 'fuel diplomacy' visits to Southeast Asian countries to secure supplies. Experts warn these are short-term fixes for a structural vulnerability, as Australia imports about 80% of its refined fuel.

Canada T20 World Cup game under ICC scrutiny after corruption claim | Cricket News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T10:18:10Z

The ICC is investigating Cricket Canada over allegations of match corruption, focusing on Canada's loss to New Zealand in a T20 World Cup game. Suspicion is centered on a specific over bowled by captain Dilpreet Bajwa. The investigation follows a CBC documentary that made wide-ranging accusations of corruption and poor governance against the national cricket body.

Displaced Lebanese are returning to southern Lebanon after a 10-day truce with Israel began, despite calls from the army to delay. Hezbollah warns it has its 'finger on the trigger' for any Israeli violations. The article reports early ceasefire violations by Israel and casualties from unexploded ordnance, while residents assess widespread destruction from over a month of attacks that killed more than 2,100 people.

IMF, World Bank say they are restoring ties with Venezuela | Business and Economy News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T03:24:11Z

The IMF and World Bank have announced the resumption of ties with Venezuela under acting President Delcy Rodriguez. The institutions had severed relations in 2019 due to a dispute over the country's leadership. The move, which follows the U.S. lifting sanctions on Rodriguez, clears the way for Venezuela to potentially request financial assistance.

The ceasefire includes Iran-backed Hezbollah and opens the door for potential talks between Israeli and Lebanese leaders.

France arrests suspect over 1982 attack on Jewish restaurant | Crime News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-17T04:08:41Z

France has arrested Mahmoud Khader Abed Adra, a suspect in a deadly 1982 attack on a Jewish restaurant in Paris, after he was surrendered by Palestinian authorities. French President Emmanuel Macron linked the handover to France's recent recognition of Palestinian statehood. The attack, blamed on a Palestinian faction, killed six people and wounded 22.

Former Australian special forces soldier Ben Roberts-Smith has been granted bail after being charged with murdering five people in Afghanistan between 2009 and 2012. A judge cited the lengthy time before trial as a reason for his release. Roberts-Smith, a decorated Victoria Cross recipient, denies all charges, which carry a maximum sentence of life imprisonment.

The company's stock fell about 8% on the news, though Netflix reaffirmed its financial outlook and highlighted areas of future growth, including advertising.

This action is part of President Trump's broader 'Donroe Doctrine' to expand U.S. influence and counter activities like enabling adversarial powers or undermining regional security.

Iran's ambassador stated Tehran would only hold talks with Washington in Pakistan, citing trust.

Mohammed Lubbad's wife Amal was killed in an Israeli air strike in October 2023 while eight months pregnant, but he was later told she had given birth via C-section. The search has become more complicated as a baby matching the description was identified and registered by another family, leading to a dispute.

This follows a separate school shooting in Sanliurfa province two days earlier, and authorities have detained dozens over alleged threatening social media posts.

Fairfax, a Democrat who faced sexual assault allegations in 2019, had largely disappeared from politics after a failed gubernatorial bid.

Overall Summary

This batch of articles covers a range of global news from the last six hours, focusing on regional impacts of the US-Israel war on Iran, domestic political and economic crises, and local humanitarian struggles. Stories span from Kashmir to Australia, highlighting community aid, electoral disputes, industrial accidents, and the daily challenges of survival in conflict zones.

Articles

Why many Kashmiris are donating gold, breaking piggy banks for Iran | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T06:56:55Z

In Indian-administered Kashmir, many people are donating personal valuables like gold, savings, and household items to aid civilians in Iran affected by the US-Israel war. This effort, driven by a centuries-old historical bond, involves contributions from both Shia and Sunni communities, with families simplifying Eid celebrations to redirect resources. The campaign has drawn recognition from Iranian authorities and some concern from Indian officials over fund collection methods.

Read original article

Muslims the target? Fury as millions lose voting rights in India’s Bengal | Elections News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T07:51:30Z

A controversial revision of electoral rolls in West Bengal, India, has removed more than nine million people from voter lists ahead of state elections. Many, like 73-year-old Nabijan Mondal, were excluded due to discrepancies between their voter card names and other official documents. Critics argue the process disproportionately affects Muslims, who constitute about 27% of the state's population, and with special tribunals unable to handle the volume of cases before polling, millions may be unable to vote.

Read original article

Fire breaks out at crucial Australian refinery, raising fuel supply fears | Business and Economy News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T08:44:46Z

A major fire caused by a gas leak has erupted at Viva Energy's oil refinery in Geelong, Australia, which produces about 10% of the nation's fuel. The incident occurs as Australia, which imports 80% of its fuel, faces supply pressures due to the Middle East conflict. Authorities have brought the blaze under control and urged against panic-buying, while the government has secured additional diesel shipments from Brunei and South Korea to mitigate shortages.

Read original article

Afghan villagers turn to gold-panning to sustain livelihoods | Mining | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T08:52:00Z

In eastern Afghanistan's Kunar province, hundreds of men are panning for gold dust in the Hindu Kush riverbeds due to scarce economic opportunities. Workers use basic methods like sieves and pans to extract small gold nuggets, with some claiming they can find up to a gram of gold worth about $125 in a week. This informal mining has provided a livelihood for over a decade in a region where Afghanistan's natural resources have been largely unexploited amid conflict.

Read original article

South African politician Julius Malema sentenced to prison for firing gun | Courts News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T09:09:56Z

South African opposition leader Julius Malema has been sentenced to five years in prison for unlawfully firing a rifle at a 2018 party rally. The magistrate convicted him on charges including unlawful possession of a firearm and discharging a weapon in a public place, despite Malema's defense that the gun was a toy and the shots were celebratory. His lawyers have been granted leave to appeal, and if upheld, the sentence would bar him from serving as a lawmaker, dealing a setback to his Economic Freedom Fighters party.

Read original article

Why are fuel price protests sweeping the Republic of Ireland? | Protests News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T09:47:35Z

Fuel price protests led by hauliers and farmers have swept the Republic of Ireland, with convoys blockading roads and infrastructure for over a week. Demonstrators cite fuel price increases of around 28% for diesel and 25% for petrol since the US-Israel war on Iran began and the Strait of Hormuz closure. The government has deployed the army to remove protesters, drawing comparisons to France's Yellow Vests movement, though protests in Northern Ireland have so far been muted.

Read original article

'Iran has to come' to the US for the World Cup, FIFA's Infantino says | World Cup 2026 News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T09:50:43Z

FIFA President Gianni Infantino insists Iran's national team will participate in the World Cup in the United States despite Iran's sports minister citing safety concerns amid the war with the US and Israel. Infantino, who visited the team in Turkey, said the players want to play and relocation of games to Mexico is not feasible. Iran is scheduled to play all its group stage matches on the US West Coast, with FIFA promising safety for the team.

Read original article

DRC and M23 rebels eye peace monitoring agreement in Switzerland | Conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T09:53:00Z

The Democratic Republic of the Congo and M23 rebels are negotiating in Switzerland, with mediation by the US and Qatar, to establish a peace monitoring mechanism. Reports indicate an interim agreement has been signed to track humanitarian and security developments and monitor ceasefire violations. These talks occur amid ongoing clashes in eastern DRC, particularly in South Kivu, where civilians are caught in the fighting despite a December peace accord.

Read original article

Sudan power crisis: Studying using candles, counting gas station trips | Sudan war News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T10:33:43Z

Sudan is experiencing crippling power cuts exacerbated by the war and global energy disruptions from the US-Israel conflict with Iran, severely impacting daily life. Residents like Husna Mohamed face challenges such as fetching water manually and studying by candlelight, while surging fuel prices and currency devaluation increase costs. The government's promise to restore services in Khartoum has proven difficult amid the ongoing conflict and economic turmoil.

Read original article

Forced to drop out: Yemen's children trade school for survival | Education News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T10:36:28Z

In Yemen, millions of children like 14-year-old Qasim have dropped out of school to work, collecting plastic bottles to help their families afford basic meals. UNICEF estimates 3.2 million school-aged children are out of school, with parents prioritizing survival over education after years of conflict. Qasim's father expresses that seeing a hungry child is more painful than seeing a child leave school, reflecting the dire economic conditions despite a ceasefire.

The shuttle diplomacy follows unsuccessful weekend talks, with mediators also addressing control of the Strait of Hormuz and wartime compensation.

The Warriors advance to a final play-in game against the Phoenix Suns, while the loss ends the Clippers' season.

Israel appoints first ambassador to Somaliland | Border Disputes News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-15T21:05:27Z

Israel has appointed its first ambassador to Somaliland, Michael Lotem, months after becoming the first country to recognize the breakaway region. Somaliland's president praised Israel as a reliable partner, while Somalia's government condemned the move as a breach of its sovereignty. The partnership has developed rapidly since December, involving diplomatic visits and discussions on trade and security cooperation, drawing wide international condemnation.

A New York jury found that Live Nation and its subsidiary Ticketmaster maintained a harmful monopoly over major concert venues. The verdict could cost the companies hundreds of millions of dollars in overcharge reimbursements and potential penalties, and may lead to court-ordered divestitures. The civil case, led by US states, accused Live Nation of smothering competition, while the company argued its success was not illegal.

Bayern knock Real Madrid out of Champions League in quarterfinal classic | Football News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-15T21:31:04Z

Bayern Munich defeated Real Madrid 4-3 in a dramatic Champions League quarterfinal, advancing 6-4 on aggregate after late goals from Luis Diaz and Michael Olise. The match saw Real Madrid take the lead three times in the first half and a red card for Eduardo Camavinga, with tempers flaring after the final whistle. Bayern will face Paris St Germain in the semifinals.

The probe centers on social media posts from January where Bolsonaro insinuated Lula was linked to crimes like drug trafficking. Bolsonaro's spokesperson denounced the investigation as a violation of his rights, while polls show a tight race between the two ahead of October's election.

Peru's presidential election vote count continues three days after the poll, with leftist candidate Roberto Sanchez moving into second place. The election was marred by logistical chaos and delays, fueling public skepticism and fraud claims from some candidates. With about 90% of votes counted, Keiko Fujimori leads, while Sanchez and Rafael Lopez Aliaga are in a tight race for the second runoff spot.

El Salvador publishes law allowing life sentences for minors as young as 12 | Crime News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-15T23:45:44Z

El Salvador has published a new law allowing life imprisonment for minors as young as 12 convicted of severe crimes like homicide or terrorism, set to take effect April 26. The measure is part of President Nayib Bukele's hardline anti-gang policies under a prolonged state of emergency. UN agencies have expressed deep concern, warning it contradicts children's rights and is unlikely to reduce crime.

Russian attack on Ukraine’s Kyiv kills 12-year-old child, wounds 10 | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T01:06:01Z

A Russian attack on Kyiv killed a 12-year-old child and wounded at least 10 people, including medics, according to the city's mayor. Rocket fragments hit a residential building in the Podilskyi district, causing a fire, while debris also sparked a large fire in the Obolonsky district. Separate attacks in Dnipro, Kharkiv, and Odessa wounded additional civilians.

Brazil’s fugitive ex-spy chief freed in the US after ICE detention | News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-16T01:18:06Z

Former Brazilian intelligence chief Alexandre Ramagem, convicted for his role in a coup plot, has been released from US immigration detention after being held by ICE in Florida. Ramagem fled Brazil before serving a 16-year sentence. Brazil has requested his extradition, but a commentator stated his detention was for a traffic infraction and that he has a pending asylum application.

Critics argue it risks disenfranchising voters, while supporters claim it boosts election security.

Even if peace is achieved in 2026, the country is projected to lose an estimated $18.8bn in GDP by 2043.

Global oil demand to plunge amid disruptions caused by war on Iran: IEA | Oil and Gas News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-14T10:59:11Z

The International Energy Agency (IEA) has sharply cut its forecasts for global oil supply and demand growth, projecting a fall of 80,000 barrels per day this year. It cites disruptions from the US-Israel war on Iran, including Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the subsequent US blockade of Iranian ports, as causing the largest oil supply disruption in history. The IEA warns that 'demand destruction' will spread due to scarcity and higher prices.

The Pakistan Cricket Board stated the move violated contractual obligations and principles of good faith.

It notes that Iran earned nearly $5 billion from oil exports in the past month while controlling the Strait of Hormuz, a 40% increase from pre-war levels. However, experts say the blockade now directly threatens Tehran's capacity to export crude oil, which accounts for about 80% of its total exports.

The FAO stresses the urgency of resuming traffic to avoid a crisis, noting poorer countries are most exposed.

Union Berlin has condemned sexist online abuse directed at Marie-Louise Eta after she became the first woman to take charge of a men's Bundesliga team as interim head coach. Club officials expressed full confidence in Eta and pushed back against derogatory comments on social media. Eta will oversee the men's team for the remainder of the season before moving to the club's women's side.

Ice hockey coach admits to faking COVID certificate to enter China Olympics | Olympics News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-14T10:39:35Z

Swiss Ice Hockey coach Patrick Fischer has admitted to using a falsified COVID-19 vaccination certificate to travel to the 2022 Winter Olympics in Beijing, circumventing China's strict travel restrictions. Fischer said he made a 'serious mistake' but did not want to let his team down. The Swiss Ice Hockey Federation considers the matter closed as Fischer is already due to step down after the upcoming World Championship.

The government also appointed Vice Admiral Mark Hammond as the new head of the Australian Defence Force.

The win also makes Sinner only the second player, after Novak Djokovic, to win the 'Sunshine Double' (Indian Wells and Miami) and Monte Carlo in the same season.

NASA's Artemis II crew has safely splashed down in the Pacific Ocean, completing the first human mission to travel around the Moon and back in over 50 years. The mission, which set several historic firsts, is seen as a critical test flight for future lunar landing plans.

They contend the law is meant for short-term monetary emergencies, not for addressing routine trade deficits, which is the administration's stated reason for the tariffs.

Myanmar’s coup leader Min Aung Hlaing sworn in as president 2026-04-10T07:36:09Z

In his inauguration address, he stated Myanmar was returning to democracy and pledged to enhance international relations, particularly with ASEAN. The election and ceremony have been widely criticized as a sham by democracy watchdogs and Western governments.

Russia and Ukraine have agreed to a 32-hour ceasefire for Orthodox Easter, beginning Saturday afternoon Moscow time. Both President Vladimir Putin and President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced the pause, with Zelenskyy stating Ukraine had proposed a similar measure. The ceasefire comes as wider diplomatic efforts remain stalled, and follows reports of continued attacks in the Dnipropetrovsk region after the announcement.

Guelleh's path was cleared after lawmakers scrapped presidential age limits last year. The election is largely uncontested, with low early turnout reported, and has been criticized by human rights groups for repressing political freedom.

Over 77% of Gaza's population faces severe acute food insecurity.

Experts note it is too early to predict a return to normal due to the complexity involving multiple regional and global powers.

This legal grey zone creates daily uncertainty and barriers, such as preventing children from enrolling in public schools.

The crew, which includes several historic firsts, will document lunar features before returning on a free-return trajectory.

Gig economy workers in Vietnam, like e-hailing drivers, are facing severe financial strain as fuel prices have skyrocketed due to the war on Iran disrupting oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. Diesel prices have more than doubled, forcing many drivers to stop working as fuel costs consume a large portion of their earnings. The Vietnamese government has suspended an environmental tax on fuel until mid-April to help stabilize prices, but the economic strain is evident with overloaded public transport and reduced domestic flights.

Read original article

The astronauts are expected to swing around the far side of the moon early this week, potentially traveling farther from Earth than any humans in history. The mission is part of NASA's broader plan to establish a permanent lunar base for deeper space exploration.

The landlocked nation, which relies almost entirely on India for fuel, has seen price hikes and supply issues. The government is also exploring legal measures to convert petrol and diesel vehicles to electric power.

The government has ramped up vaccination efforts in the worst-affected areas, though a previous vaccination drive was delayed due to political unrest.

Iranian forces claim they struck down two US fighter jets, an F-15 over southwest Iran and an A-10 near the Strait of Hormuz. The fate of at least one pilot from the F-15 crew is unknown, with reports that one was rescued but another remains missing. US President Trump said the downing would not affect the prospect of talks with Tehran, stating 'We're in war.' Iranian officials have called on civilians to be on the lookout for survivors.

Read original article

Ukraine slows enemy advances, liberates land, drains Russia’s war chest | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T15:44:16Z

Ukraine has devastated Russia's oil export capacity, stopped Russian advances, and reclaimed occupied territory, with one estimate saying it halved the Russian rate of advance in three months. President Zelenskyy has secured agreements with several Gulf states to export Ukrainian drone know-how in return for joint production support. Commander-in-chief Syrskii said the rate of drone production is key to Ukraine's success, with first-person view drones now responsible for 90% of Russian casualties, and Ukraine has reclaimed 470 sq km of territory this year.

Read original article

Trump seeks historic $1.5 trillion for military in Congress budget request | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T15:48:57Z

President Trump has requested a $1.5 trillion military budget for the next fiscal year, a nearly 40% increase over the previous year, which would be the largest in decades. The request, which is not legally binding, emphasizes military spending and law enforcement while proposing $73bn in cuts to domestic programmes like climate initiatives and refugee aid. The budget is separate from a previous $200bn emergency request for the US-Israeli war with Iran and is expected to face lengthy congressional negotiations.

Read original article

Omani, French and Japanese vessels transit the Strait of Hormuz | News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T16:24:00Z

A French container ship, three Oman-linked tankers, and a Japanese-owned gas carrier have crossed the Strait of Hormuz, with the French vessel being the first Western ship known to do so since Iran effectively closed the strait. It was not immediately clear how the vessels secured safe passage; the French ship appeared to signal its owner's nationality before crossing. Only about 150 vessels have transited the strait since March 1, most linked to Iran, China, India, and Pakistan.

Read original article

US unemployment rate drops despite economic uncertainty and Iran war | Business and Economy News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T17:35:23Z

The US unemployment rate fell to 4.3% in March as non-farm payrolls grew by 178,000 jobs, rebounding from a revised loss in February. The biggest gains were in healthcare (adding 76,000 jobs, partly due to a nursing strike ending) and construction (adding 26,000 jobs), while the federal government cut 18,000 positions. The White House praised the report as evidence of Trump's economic policies working, though experts caution the impact of the Iran war is not yet reflected in job numbers.

Read original article

Cambodia parliament approves law to combat cybercrime scam rings | News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T17:40:45Z

Cambodia's National Assembly has approved its first law specifically targeting scam centres accused of defrauding foreigners of billions of dollars. The law sets prison sentences of 2-5 years and fines up to $125,000 for online scams, with higher penalties for gang activity or human trafficking, including up to 20 years in prison. Justice Minister Keut Rith said the law aims to enhance a 'cleaning operation' and send a message that Cambodia is not a place for scams.

Read original article

Drone strike on Sudan hospital kills 10, medical charity MSF says | News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T18:32:43Z

A drone attack on Al Jabalain Hospital in Sudan's White Nile State killed at least 10 people, including seven medical staff, according to Doctors Without Borders (MSF). The attack, which MSF said was 'reportedly' carried out by the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), hit an operating theatre and a maternity ward during a children's immunisation campaign. This is part of a series of attacks on healthcare in Sudan's ongoing war, with over 200 attacks on facilities reported since the conflict began.

At least one killed at UAE’s Habshan gas facility after intercepted attack | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T19:08:52Z

Debris from an intercepted attack caused fires at the UAE's Habshan gas facility, killing one Egyptian national and injuring four others. The Abu Dhabi Media Office said the debris fell after a successful interception by air defence systems, causing 'significant damage' to the country's largest natural gas processing site. The incident occurred amid intense Iranian missile and drone attacks on the UAE, with officials reporting at least 18 ballistic missiles, 4 cruise missiles, and 47 drones fired from Iran over 24 hours.

More than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1: WHO | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T19:16:56Z

The World Health Organization (WHO) has verified over 20 attacks on healthcare facilities in Iran since March 1, resulting in at least nine deaths. WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus cited damage to the Pasteur Institute in Tehran, which was rendered unable to continue health services, and attacks on other facilities including a psychiatric hospital and a pharmaceutical plant. The attacks appear to be part of a widening of US-Israeli targets beyond military and political sites to include health, education, and transport infrastructure.

US judge upholds decision to toss subpoenas into Fed Chair Jerome Powell | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T19:24:56Z

A US federal judge has again rejected subpoenas from the Trump administration seeking information about Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, stating the government's arguments 'do not come close to convincing the Court' of their legitimacy. Judge James Boasberg previously nullified the subpoenas in March, ruling they were issued for an 'improper purpose' to pressure Powell into compliance with Trump's demands. The administration has focused on cost overruns in Fed building renovations as evidence of malfeasance, which Powell has called a 'pretext' to undermine the central bank's independence.

Read original article

Two dozen Democrat-led states sue Trump over mail-in ballot limits | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T19:28:35Z

About two dozen Democrat-led states and the District of Columbia have filed a lawsuit to block a Trump executive order that sets new limits on mail-in ballots. The order requires the Department of Homeland Security to compile lists of eligible voters and the Postal Service to send ballots only to those on state-specific lists. The states argue the order violates the US Constitution by interfering with state authority over elections and could create chaos ahead of November's midterm elections.

How war on Iran turned Pakistan’s LNG surplus into a looming shortage | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T04:55:17Z

Pakistan is facing a looming gas shortage crisis after starting the year with an LNG surplus. This shift occurred after the US-Israel war on Iran led to attacks on key gas facilities in Qatar and Iran, disrupting supplies through the Strait of Hormuz. As a result, Qatar halted LNG production and declared force majeure, severely impacting Pakistan, which relies heavily on imports from Qatar and the UAE with no emergency reserves.

Read original article

How China’s ‘teapot’ refineries are cushioning it from Iran war oil crisis | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T05:57:07Z

China's small independent 'teapot' refineries are helping cushion the country from the oil crisis caused by the war on Iran and the paralysis of the Strait of Hormuz. These refineries had stockpiled oil from countries like Iran, Russia, and Venezuela, capitalizing on cheap oil due to sanctions. While China's oil supply is not entirely immune, its seaborne crude imports in March remained broadly in line with 2025 averages, though most arrivals were loaded before the conflict began.

Read original article

Myanmar’s coup leader elected president by pro-military parliament | Politics News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-03T06:05:30Z

Myanmar's coup leader, Min Aung Hlaing, has been elected president by the country's pro-military parliament, formalizing his political power five years after ousting an elected government. He received at least 293 votes, passing the majority threshold, following a lopsided election won by an army-backed party. His ascent to the presidency, after a military leadership reshuffle, is seen as a strategic move to consolidate power under a nominally civilian government while a civil war continues to rage.

SpaceX files to go public, paving way to make Elon Musk first trillionaire 2026-04-01T21:12:27Z

SpaceX has taken steps for its first public share offering, which could become one of the largest IPOs in modern history and potentially make founder Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. The company, considered the most valuable privately held firm, filed for an IPO that could raise up to $75bn and take place as soon as June or July. Despite Musk's controversial public profile, analysts note SpaceX is operationally mature, technologically advanced, and profitable, which may drive investor enthusiasm.

US removes sanctions on Venezuela’s interim President Delcy Rodriguez 2026-04-01T22:12:46Z

The United States has lifted sanctions against Venezuela's interim President Delcy Rodriguez, following the abduction and imprisonment of her predecessor Nicolas Maduro. Rodriguez hailed the decision as a step toward normalizing relations and called for more sanctions to be lifted. Since taking office, Rodriguez has taken a conciliatory approach toward the US, including signing laws to open Venezuela's oil and mining sectors to private investment.

Read original article

NASA successfully launches historic Artemis II moon mission 2026-04-01T22:39:26Z

NASA's Artemis II mission has successfully launched from Florida, sending four astronauts on a journey around the moon—the first human travel beyond low-Earth orbit in over 50 years. The crew will conduct a nearly 10-day mission, performing extensive systems checks before a critical engine burn to head toward the moon. The launch proceeded despite pre-launch tensions over hydrogen fuel leaks and technical issues with the flight-termination system and capsule batteries, which were resolved.

Read original article

First troops from UN-backed Gang Suppression Force arrive in Haiti 2026-04-01T23:19:08Z

The first foreign troops from a new UN-backed Gang Suppression Force have arrived in Haiti to combat escalating gang violence, with an initial group from Chad deploying alongside a UN special representative. The force, authorized by the UN Security Council, is expected to grow to 5,500 personnel with a 12-month mandate and will have powers to make arrests, unlike the previous Kenyan-led mission. The deployment follows a history of troubled foreign interventions in Haiti and comes amid widespread violence that intensified after the 2021 assassination of President Jovenel Moise.

Read original article

FBI agents from US arrive in Cuba to probe lethal speedboat shooting 2026-04-01T23:31:08Z

A technical team from the FBI has arrived in Cuba to conduct an independent investigation into a deadly shootout between Cuban border patrol and a Florida-tagged speedboat carrying armed individuals. Cuban authorities say 10 Cuban nationals attempted to enter the island with weapons and ammunition on February 25, resulting in five deaths. The US embassy stated the investigation will verify facts independently, amid heightened tensions following a US oil blockade on Cuba and the abduction of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, a key Cuban ally.

Read original article

Magnitude 7.4 quake hits off Indonesia’s Ternate, tsunami warning lifted 2026-04-01T23:54:08Z

A magnitude 7.4 earthquake struck off the coast of Ternate, Indonesia, killing at least one person and initially triggering a tsunami warning that was later lifted. The quake, which occurred at a depth of 35km, caused building damage and led to evacuation preparations in some cities. Tsunami waves up to 75 centimeters were recorded in some areas, but the Pacific Tsunami Warning Center stated the threat had passed about two hours after the tremor.

The census is scheduled to conclude next year.

The dominant theme is the ongoing US-Israel war on Iran, with updates on military strikes, diplomatic stances, and regional fallout. Other major stories include sports news related to World Cup qualifiers and incidents of racism, significant political developments in India, and the imminent launch of NASA's Artemis II moon mission.

Articles

Iran war: What is happening on day 33 of US-Israel attacks? | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T07:01:14Z

On day 33 of the conflict, US-Israeli air attacks continue across Iran, targeting industrial and civilian sites including pharmaceutical plants, steel facilities, and a desalination plant. US President Donald Trump stated the war could end in two to three weeks without a deal, but Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tehran has 'zero' trust in talks with Washington. The article also notes NATO allies restricting support and China pushing for a ceasefire.

Read original article

Spain’s FA condemns Islamophobic chants during game with Egypt | Football News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T07:43:14Z

The Royal Spanish Football Federation and coach Luis de la Fuente have condemned anti-Muslim chants that marred a pre-World Cup friendly against Egypt in Barcelona. Egypt's national anthem was jeered, and the public announcement system repeatedly appealed to fans to stop the offensive comments. This incident is described as part of a string of similar racist and Islamophobic incidents in Spanish football in recent years.

Read original article

What is NASA’s Artemis II moon mission, and when will it launch? | Space News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T07:51:58Z

NASA's Artemis II mission is set to launch, sending four astronauts on a lunar flyby, marking the first crewed mission to the moon since 1972. The launch window opens on April 1, with weather being the primary constraint after previous technical delays. The mission's goal is to test the Orion spacecraft's life support and other systems in deep space as part of NASA's broader programme to return humans to the moon.

Read original article

Iran attacks cause fire in Kuwait, Bahrain; kill man in UAE | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T07:54:12Z

Iranian drone attacks have caused a large fire at fuel tanks in Kuwait's international airport and a fire at a company facility in Bahrain. In the UAE, shrapnel from a drone interception killed a Bangladeshi national. The article also reports a tanker was hit by a projectile near Qatar, with Qatar's defence ministry stating it was targeted by Iranian cruise missiles.

War on Iran: Three key takeaways from Araghchi’s interview with Al Jazeera | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T08:27:51Z

In an interview, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi confirmed direct contact with US envoy Steve Witkoff but denied it constituted negotiations, stating Iran has 'zero' trust in the US. He argued that Iran and Oman would decide the future of the Strait of Hormuz after the war. Araghchi also referenced Iran's preparations for a potential US ground invasion.

‘Gerrymandering’ in India’s Assam cuts Muslim representation before vote | Politics | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T08:46:49Z

A redrawing of electoral constituency boundaries in Assam state has significantly reduced the number of seats where Muslims form a majority, down from about 35 to about 20 out of 126. Analysts and activists describe this as 'communal gerrymandering' that could politically marginalise Assam's Muslim population ahead of state elections on April 9. The ruling BJP has chosen Hindu candidates for constituencies that previously had a chance of electing Muslim representatives.

Read original article

India begins world’s largest population census | Politics News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T08:47:21Z

India has begun a yearlong national population census involving over three million officials, which will collect data on housing, living conditions, and later, social and economic characteristics including caste and religion. The data could reshape welfare programmes and political representation, potentially leading to a redrawing of India's political map. This is the first census since 2011, and India is now estimated to be the world's most populous nation.

Read original article

‘An incredible feeling’: Turkiye beat Kosovo to end 24-year World Cup wait | Football News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T09:06:41Z

Turkiye has qualified for the FIFA World Cup for the first time since 2002 after a tense 1-0 playoff win against Kosovo. The victory, secured by a Kerem Akturkoglu goal, prompted wild celebrations among players and fans. Coach Vincenzo Montella expressed pride in his squad, which blends emerging young stars with seasoned players.

Which teams are in the FIFA World Cup 2026? | World Cup 2026 News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T09:33:49Z

The 48-team lineup for the 2026 FIFA World Cup is now complete following the final playoffs, with Iraq and the Democratic Republic of the Congo among the last to qualify. The tournament, hosted by Canada, Mexico, and the US, will be the largest ever, featuring debutants like Cape Verde and Uzbekistan. The article provides a breakdown of the teams and groups, noting Argentina will defend its title.

Israeli attacks on Beirut's southern suburbs and the town of Khaldeh have killed at least seven people, according to Lebanon's health ministry. The Israeli military said it aimed to kill a senior Hezbollah commander. Meanwhile, Hezbollah claimed cross-border attacks and said its fighters were engaged in clashes with Israeli soldiers in southern Lebanon, where Israel has launched a ground invasion.

Turkiye and Sweden book World Cup 2026 spots as Kosovo and Poland bow out | Football News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:04:03Z

Turkiye qualified for the World Cup for the first time since 2002 by beating Kosovo 1-0, ending a 24-year wait. Sweden secured their spot by defeating Poland 3-2, with Viktor Gyokeres scoring a late winner. Sweden will join Group F alongside the Netherlands, Japan, and Tunisia for the 2026 tournament.

FBI agents who investigated Trump file lawsuit alleging retaliatory firing | Courts News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:17:50Z

Three former FBI agents have filed a class-action lawsuit alleging they were wrongfully fired in retaliation for their work on an investigation into Donald Trump's efforts to stay in power after the 2020 election. They claim their termination, ordered by FBI Director Kash Patel, was part of a 'retribution campaign' and violated their due process. The agents had been assigned to support special counsel Jack Smith's investigation, which led to Trump's 2023 indictment before being dropped after his re-election.

Read original article

'I need to leave': Cuba's tourism workers reel from US oil blockade | Tourism News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:24:49Z

Cuba's tourism industry has collapsed due to a de facto US oil blockade imposed in January, leaving workers like taxi drivers and tour guides struggling with little paid work. Tourism, once a pillar of Cuba's economy, has seen visitor numbers plummet from a peak of 4.8 million in 2018 to 1.6 million last year. Workers report the blockade has created an economic crisis, with many considering leaving the island as they can no longer afford basic necessities.

Read original article

Russian tanker reaches Cuba amid critical energy shortage | Oil and Gas News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:25:56Z

A Russian oil tanker, the Anatoly Kolodkin, has delivered 730,000 barrels of oil to Cuba, the first such shipment in three months, to address a critical energy shortage. The US allowed the sanctioned vessel to proceed for humanitarian reasons. The fuel could meet Cuba's energy needs for up to 10 days, providing temporary relief from blackouts that have crippled hospitals, transport, and farm production.

Read original article

US Supreme Court rejects Colorado ban on LGBTQ child 'conversion therapy' | Courts News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:45:57Z

The US Supreme Court, in an 8-1 decision, ruled against a Colorado law banning 'conversion therapy' for LGBTQ children, arguing it violates free speech protections. The majority held that states cannot limit what a therapist discusses with a patient, even if aimed at changing a child's LGBTQ identity. Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson dissented, warning the decision impairs states' ability to regulate medical care and overlooks the practice's documented harms.

Read original article

Italy out as Bosnia and Herzegovina reach World Cup 2026 on penalties | World Cup 2026 News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:52:32Z

Bosnia and Herzegovina defeated Italy 4-1 on penalties after a 1-1 draw in extra time, eliminating the four-time champions from World Cup 2026 qualification. Italy, reduced to 10 men in the first half, will miss a third consecutive World Cup finals. Bosnia secured a spot in Group B, marking their second World Cup appearance and first since 2014.

Tiger Woods pleads not guilty to DUI in car crash where opioids found | Football News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T21:54:47Z

Golfer Tiger Woods pleaded not guilty to driving under the influence charges in Florida. A sheriff's report stated he showed signs of impairment at the crash scene, with bloodshot eyes, dilated pupils, and opioid pills in his pocket. Deputies reported his movements were slow and lethargic, and he told them he had taken prescription medication before the accident.

Read original article

US exempts Gulf of Mexico oil drillers from endangered species protections | Environment News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T23:02:56Z

A Trump administration committee has unanimously exempted Gulf of Mexico oil and gas drillers from Endangered Species Act protections, citing national security and energy competitiveness amid the war on Iran. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth argued the rules could hobble US energy production. Environmental groups plan to challenge the decision, which affects species like the critically endangered Rice's whale.

After Strait of Hormuz opens, turmoil would still last months, analysts say | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T23:03:49Z

Analysts warn that even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens soon, global supply chain disruptions will persist for months due to a backlog of about 2,000 stranded ships and damaged regional infrastructure. Shipping experts note it will take time to clear the accumulated oil, gas, and goods, with logistics facilities operating at full capacity. The effective shutdown has forced diversions and caused significant delays in energy and trade flows.

Read original article

DR Congo beat Jamaica to reach World Cup via intercontinental finals | World Cup 2026 News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T23:52:44Z

The Democratic Republic of the Congo qualified for the World Cup by beating Jamaica 1-0 in extra time of an intercontinental playoff, with former Manchester United defender Axel Tuanzebe scoring the decisive goal. This marks only the DRC's second World Cup appearance, their first since 1974. They will join Group G alongside Portugal, Colombia, and Uzbekistan for the 2026 tournament.

Trump tells allies 'get your own oil', says Iran war could end in 2-3 weeks | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T00:14:19Z

President Donald Trump stated the US could stop attacking Iran within two to three weeks and that no deal is necessary to end the war, despite Iranian officials denying any negotiations are occurring. Trump claimed the US would leave once Iran is set back 'into the Stone Ages' and unable to develop nuclear weapons. His comments come as domestic US petrol prices surpass $4 a gallon due to the conflict's disruption of energy supplies.

Judge temporarily halts Trump's $400m White House ballroom project | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-04-01T00:50:22Z

A US district judge has temporarily halted President Donald Trump's $400m White House ballroom project, ruling construction cannot proceed without Congressional authorization. Judge Richard Leon stated the president does not own the White House and exceeded his authority by demolishing the historic East Wing. The ruling pauses the project while a lawsuit by the National Trust for Historic Preservation continues, though the Trump administration has filed an appeal.

A UNDP report estimates the US-Israel war on Iran caused a regional GDP decline of 3.7 to 6 percent in one month, equivalent to $120bn to $194bn. The conflict is projected to lead to 3.7 million lost jobs and push about four million more people into poverty, with impacts concentrated in the Levant and fragile states like Sudan and Yemen. The report warns Lebanon is especially impacted due to ongoing air strikes and large-scale displacement.

Strikes damaged a major power line in Tehran and targeted a key pharmaceuticals company, among other industrial and civilian sites. Iranian authorities say more than 2,000 people have been killed since the war began on February 28, with residential areas, schools, hospitals, and historical sites impacted.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi confirmed messages have been exchanged with the US, including directly with envoy Steve Witkoff, but denied negotiations are taking place. Israel’s parliament passes death penalty bill targeting Palestinians | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-30T20:50:36Z

Israel's parliament passed a bill instructing military courts to impose the death penalty on Palestinians convicted of killing Israelis in acts of 'terror', but it does not apply the same penalty to Jewish Israelis convicted of killing Palestinians. The law, championed by far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir, was approved by 62 lawmakers. The Palestinian Authority called it a 'war crime' and a breach of the Fourth Geneva Convention.

The war in Iran: Key takeaways from Al Jazeera’s interview with Marco Rubio | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-30T21:03:25Z

In an interview, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said talks with Iran are ongoing through intermediaries and that the US military campaign will continue until Tehran abandons its nuclear and missile programs. He warned the Strait of Hormuz will be kept open 'one way or another' and said war objectives could be achieved 'in weeks, not months'. Rubio also stated the US would welcome political change in Iran but it is not an official objective.

Far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir celebrated the law's passage, rejecting international pressure.

At least 70 killed, 30 wounded in Haiti gang attack, rights group says | Crime News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-31T00:02:55Z

A human rights group says at least 70 people were killed and 30 injured in a gang attack in Haiti's Artibonite region, a figure significantly higher than official estimates of about 16 dead. The attack, which began Sunday and continued into Monday, involved gang members storming rural communities and setting homes on fire. The rights group estimated 6,000 people were displaced, and police said gang members fled the area before their arrival.

Analysts note that while the Houthis are Iranian allies, their decision-making is largely independent, and their entry into the conflict could open a new regional front, though some describe their current participation as 'token'.

Israeli police block Catholic cardinal from Holy Sepulchre on Palm Sunday | Religion News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-29T15:26:29Z

Israeli police prevented Cardinal Pierbattista Pizzaballa, the Latin patriarch of Jerusalem, from entering the Church of the Holy Sepulchre to mark Palm Sunday Mass. Israeli police said all holy sites in Jerusalem were closed due to safety concerns amid the US-Israel war on Iran. The Catholic Church condemned the action as a 'grave precedent', and Italian and French leaders criticized the decision as an offence to religious freedom.

With municipality electricity absent for two years, alternatives like solar lamps and systems have become prohibitively expensive for most residents.

Sabalenka defeats Gauff to win second straight Miami Open title | Tennis News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-29T05:25:00Z

Pakistan's foreign minister described the deal as a meaningful step and a 'harbinger of peace,' addressing his announcement to senior US and Iranian officials. The strait's closure has stranded thousands of vessels and driven up oil prices, with Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps controlling passage through a checkpoint system.

Iran has qualified for the World Cup but has asked for its matches to be moved from the US to Mexico over safety concerns after a warning from US President Donald Trump.

Russia declares protagonist of Oscar-winning documentary a ‘foreign agent’ | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-27T16:37:59Z

Russia has declared Pavel Talankin, the teacher and main protagonist of the Oscar-winning documentary 'Mr Nobody Against Putin,' a foreign agent. The justice ministry stated he disseminated inaccurate information about Russia's leadership and spoke against the war in Ukraine. A Russian court also banned the documentary from several streaming platforms, saying it promoted negative attitudes about the government and the war.

FBI director Kash Patel’s emails, photos hacked by Iran-linked group | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-27T17:15:39Z

An Iran-linked hacking group called the Handala Hack Team claims to have successfully accessed the personal emails of FBI Director Kash Patel, sharing photographs and documents online. The breach, confirmed by Reuters and CNN, appears to have released documents over a decade old, including travel correspondence and personal photos. The group, described as pro-Palestinian hacking vigilantes linked to Iranian cyberintelligence, also claimed credit for a recent cyberattack on medical device company Stryker as retaliation for a US-Israeli strike in Iran.

US diplomat Marco Rubio denounces settler violence, tolls in Hormuz strait | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-27T18:34:28Z

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio, departing from a G7 meeting, denounced Iran's reported plans to implement a tolling system in the Strait of Hormuz, calling it illegal and dangerous. He reiterated President Donald Trump's call for other countries to participate in securing the vital waterway for global oil supply. Rubio stated the US is prepared to be part of a plan to confront this, urging G7 members and Asian countries to contribute.

Canada’s Bill C-12 an ‘attack on refugee, migrant rights’: Advocates | Migration News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-27T19:02:50Z

More than two dozen human rights organizations in Canada have condemned the newly passed Bill C-12, calling it a significant attack on refugee and migrant rights. They warn the law will put thousands at risk by limiting access to refugee protection, enabling mass cancellation of immigration documents, and facilitating broad sharing of personal information. The government defends the legislation as an effort to reduce pressure on a strained immigration system and bolster border security.

US Palestinian activist says FBI foiled assassination plot against her | Crime News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-27T19:15:16Z

US law enforcement agencies announced they foiled a plot against Palestinian activist Nerdeen Kiswani, arresting a suspect for allegedly planning to throw Molotov cocktails at her New York home. Kiswani said the FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force informed her of the threat, which was thwarted by an undercover operation. The suspect, identified as Andrew Heifler, was charged with making and possessing destructive devices.

House Republicans shoot down bill that would pay federal airport workers | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-27T20:10:31Z

Republican leaders in the US House of Representatives rejected a Senate-passed bill that would have resumed funding for Department of Homeland Security agencies like the TSA, whose workers have gone unpaid during a partial government shutdown. House Speaker Mike Johnson called the bill a 'joke' because it withheld funding from border patrol and ICE, suggesting the House would advance its own bill. Separately, President Donald Trump signed a memo directing DHS to find a way to pay TSA employees, blaming Democrats for the impasse.
