Articles

Australia’s post-Bondi crackdown accused of targeting pro-Palestinian voices 2026-03-27T02:31:23Z

Human rights groups have criticized Australia's new hate speech laws, saying they will have a 'chilling effect' on support for the Palestinian cause. The laws, rushed through after a deadly attack in Bondi Beach, specifically target speech considered anti-Semitic and carry penalties of up to two years imprisonment. A senior lawyer expressed concern that the broad legislation could suppress legitimate criticism of Israel's actions in Gaza and be used against human rights groups.

Read original article

Trump’s signature to appear on US currency in first for sitting president 2026-03-27T03:49:41Z

President Donald Trump's signature will appear on US currency to mark the 250th anniversary of Independence Day, a first for a sitting president. The Treasury Secretary said the change recognizes Trump's 'historic achievements' and the country's economic path. The move has drawn criticism from some who liken it to the behavior of dictators, coming a week after approval of a commemorative gold coin bearing Trump's image.

Read original article

Malaysia’s ships allowed to pass Strait of Hormuz, PM Anwar says 2026-03-27T04:24:39Z

Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim says Iran has granted Malaysian ships clearance to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has effectively closed. Anwar thanked Iran's president for the 'early clearance' and said Malaysia is securing the release of oil tankers and workers. The government is introducing fuel conservation measures, including reducing subsidized petrol quotas, as the global energy crunch affects the country despite its status as a net energy exporter.

Read original article

Sri Lanka braces for new economic crisis as war on Iran continues 2026-03-27T05:34:09Z

Sri Lanka has reintroduced a QR-based fuel rationing system similar to one used during its 2022 economic crisis, due to disruptions from the war on Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz. The country imports 60% of its energy needs through the strait and has raised fuel prices by approximately 33%. Experts warn of significant food price increases as nearly half the world's urea fertilizer also passes through the strait, creating major challenges for the current government.

Read original article

The Strait of Hormuz is not just an oil chokepoint 2026-03-27T05:36:20Z

The article argues the Strait of Hormuz is the 'aortic valve of globalised production' and a single point of failure for global order, not merely an oil chokepoint. It carries vital materials including urea for fertilizers, aluminum, helium for semiconductors, and petrochemicals. The piece provides historical context, noting how control of the strait has been contested for centuries by various powers including Persians, Portuguese, British, and now Iran.

Read original article

‘Truly junk’: E-waste from rich nations floods local markets in Nigeria 2026-03-27T05:50:22Z

Nigeria faces a growing e-waste crisis as developed countries export discarded, near-end-of-life electronics to its markets. Around 60,000 tonnes of used electronics enter Nigeria annually through key ports, with many items already damaged or toxic. The trade, largely from European countries and the US, often violates international restrictions like the Basel Convention, worsening environmental and health hazards across West Africa.

Director-General Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala stated the old world order is not returning amid global trade disruptions. Key divisions exist, with the US criticizing the 'most-favoured nation' principle while China defends it, and the EU seeks a more flexible framework.

Olympics align with Trump as transgender athletes banned from women’s games | Olympics News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T16:27:43Z

The International Olympic Committee (IOC) has adopted a new policy banning transgender women from competing in women's events at the Olympics, aligning with a U.S. executive order. The policy, which will be enforced starting with the 2028 Los Angeles Games, limits eligibility to 'biological females' as determined by a mandatory gene test. IOC President Kirsty Coventry stated the move aims to protect fairness in the female category, and the policy also restricts athletes with differences in sex development (DSD).

Details revealed of Board of Peace plan for Gaza disarmament | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T16:47:01Z

A plan submitted by Board of Peace Director General Nickolay Mladenov details a proposed eight-month, multiphase process for the disarmament of Hamas and other Palestinian groups in Gaza. The plan calls for disarmament to proceed in parallel with Israel fulfilling obligations, including allowing reconstruction materials and increased humanitarian aid into Gaza, and transferring administration to a national committee. Hamas has historically refused to disarm while Israeli occupation continues, and Palestinian experts have suggested the plan amounts to the 'political surrender' of Hamas.

The Metropolitan Police confirmed an off-duty officer was involved in an incident where a group of residents surrounded and intimidated Al Jazeera journalists reporting on a suspected arson attack in North London. Video footage shows men shouting at the crew, with one identified as a police officer using derogatory language. The police stated they are reviewing the footage to identify any offences and emphasized that journalists must be able to work without intimidation.

More than one million displaced by Israel’s evacuations in Lebanon | Israel attacks Lebanon News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T17:40:31Z

Israel has issued expanded evacuation orders for Lebanese residents south of the Zahrani River, threatening those who stay. The orders, covering about 14% of Lebanon's territory, have displaced over one million people—more than 18% of the population—within two weeks. The humanitarian crisis is severe, with many in overcrowded shelters or sleeping outdoors, and over 250,000 people have fled the country, including thousands of Lebanese citizens crossing into Syria.

U.S. President Donald Trump claimed Iran is 'begging to make a deal' to end the war, while Iran has formally responded to a U.S. proposal by asserting its rights over the Strait of Hormuz and issuing new demands. Iran's conditions, reported by the Tasnim news agency, include an end to assassinations, war reparations, and cessation of hostilities from regional resistance groups. A U.S. special envoy suggested Iran was seeking an 'off-ramp' for negotiations, but an Iranian source accused the U.S. of deceptive tactics.

US judge weighs Trump decision to bar Venezuelan funds for Maduro’s defence | Nicolas Maduro News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T19:53:04Z

A U.S. judge declined to dismiss drug-trafficking and weapons charges against former Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and his wife but questioned the Trump administration's decision to bar Venezuela from funding their legal defence. Judge Alvin Hellerstein challenged prosecutors' national security argument, noting Maduro poses no threat while imprisoned and emphasizing the constitutional right to a vigorous defence. Maduro and his wife pleaded not guilty in January after being taken into U.S. custody.

Dutch court bans xAI’s Grok from generating nonconsensual nude images | Technology News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T19:56:31Z

A Dutch court ordered Elon Musk's xAI to stop its Grok AI tool from generating and distributing nonconsensual nude images in the Netherlands, threatening daily fines for noncompliance. The ruling came in a civil suit brought by a monitoring center, which demonstrated the tool could still create such imagery despite company measures. The court placed the burden on xAI to prevent its tools from being used to create sexualized deepfakes.

Israel sends more troops into southern Lebanon as ground invasion expands | Israel attacks Lebanon News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T20:10:30Z

Israel is deploying more troops from Division 162 into southern Lebanon to expand a 'buffer zone,' joining two other divisions already operating there. The move follows Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's statement about creating a larger zone to counter Hezbollah's missile threat. Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam warned the invasion threatens Lebanon's sovereignty and violates international law, stating his government would submit a complaint to the UN Security Council.

Legal experts describe the prosecution of a former head of state as largely uncharted territory, with many questions about evidence and legal strategy unanswered.

Jury finds Meta, YouTube liable for social media addiction: What we know | Social Media News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T11:59:01Z

A Los Angeles jury found Alphabet's Google and Meta liable in a landmark civil trial over youth social media addiction. The plaintiff, a 20-year-old woman, testified that addictive platform features exacerbated her depression, suicidal thoughts, and body dysmorphia. The jury recommended the companies pay $6m in damages, though TikTok and Snapchat had previously settled their parts in the case.

Both nations are close allies of Russia and have backed its war in Ukraine.

NBA owners vote to explore Seattle, Las Vegas expansion bids | Basketball News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T06:13:47Z

The vote allows the league to formally explore these opportunities, with bids expected to cost between $7bn and $10bn per team. If expansion proceeds, it would be the NBA's most significant since 2004, and a Western Conference team would likely move east to balance the leagues.

Helium hitch: Why US-Israel war on Iran could cause MRI scan delays | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T08:51:30Z

The war has disrupted about one-third of global helium supplies, critical for MRI scanners and semiconductors, largely due to shipping restrictions through the Strait of Hormuz and halted production in Qatar. Qatar, a major producer, exports most of its helium via the Strait, where traffic has nearly halted following Iranian warnings. The shortage is expected to have ripple effects across medical and high-tech industries.

‘He will call me Mama’: The Gaza ‘grandmother’ raising an orphaned baby | Women | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T08:53:33Z

The article profiles Maha al-Rubaie, a 56-year-old woman in Gaza who is raising her orphaned grandnephew, Hamza. Hamza's parents and siblings were killed in Israeli airstrikes during the war, and he was delivered via emergency caesarean after his mother's death. Maha, who had previously raised Hamza's father after he was orphaned, now cares for the baby in a former classroom, referring to herself as his 'Mama'.

Maduro and his wife, who were abducted by US forces in January and remain jailed, plead not guilty to all charges, which they deny as part of an imperialist plot. Dozens dead in Bangladesh as passenger bus plunges into Padma River | News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T10:24:30Z

Malaysia moves to tighten rules for expats, raising fears of talent flight | Business and Economy News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T02:40:43Z

Malaysia's government plans to reduce reliance on foreign workers by raising the minimum salary threshold for visas and capping length of stay, aiming to encourage local hiring and boost incomes. The move has created uncertainty for long-term expatriates, including highly-paid professionals, who contribute significantly to the economy. The policy is part of a national strategy citing that reliance on low-skilled foreign labor has hampered technology adoption and wage growth.

Read original article

Australia bans visitors from Iran amid war in the Middle East | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T03:28:18Z

Australia has imposed a six-month ban on Iranian visitors, citing the conflict in Iran increases the risk they may not depart when their visas expire. The ban applies to Iranian citizens outside Australia, even with existing visas, with exceptions for family members of citizens and permanent residents. The government describes the move as in the national interest, while a refugee support group criticizes it for blocking a pathway to safety.

Read original article

US lawmakers push for pause in data centres until AI safeguards in place | Technology News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T03:39:28Z

US lawmakers Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez have introduced legislation calling for a moratorium on new AI data center construction until national safeguards are established. They argue a pause is needed to address potential harms to workers, civil liberties, and the environment from the rapid AI rollout. The proposal follows grassroots opposition to data centers over environmental and economic concerns, though the bill faces a challenging path in Congress.

Oil prices rise higher as Iran denies US talks, dimming deescalation hopes | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-26T05:06:02Z

Oil prices increased, with Brent crude surpassing $104 a barrel, after Iran denied reports of direct talks with the US, reducing hopes for de-escalation in the conflict. The price rise follows a previous easing on reports of a US peace plan. Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz and attacks on energy facilities have driven prices up over 40% since the war began, with shipping transits through the strait drastically reduced.

US Southern Command stated the vessel was operated by a designated terrorist organization and was engaged in narco-trafficking. The attack is part of Operation Southern Spear, a boat-bombing campaign that has reportedly killed approximately 163 people since September and has been criticized by legal experts as extrajudicial killing.

A consortium including former Walmart chairman Rob Walton bought the Rajasthan Royals for $1.63 billion, and a group led by David Blitzer's Bolt Ventures and Blackstone bought the Royal Challengers Bengaluru for $1.78 billion. These deals highlight the growing international investment appeal of India's premier cricket league.

UN passes resolution naming slave trade ‘gravest crime against humanity’ | Slavery News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T18:55:28Z

The UN General Assembly has adopted a non-binding resolution, proposed by Ghana, that recognizes the transatlantic slave trade as the 'gravest crime against humanity' and calls for reparations. The resolution passed with 123 votes in favor, 3 against (including the US and Israel), and 52 abstentions (including the UK and EU countries). It urges member states to engage in dialogue on reparatory justice, including formal apologies and financial compensation.

The plaintiff, who began using YouTube and Instagram as a child, claimed the features exacerbated her mental health issues. The companies argued her issues had other causes, but the jury found social media was a 'substantial factor' in the harm.

US President Trump claims negotiations with Iran are underway, but Iranian officials deny this, and there are reports of a US-delivered 15-point peace plan. The conflict is disrupting global energy markets, particularly due to issues in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran has appointed former IRGC commander Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr as the new head of the Supreme National Security Council, replacing Ali Larijani who was killed in an airstrike. Zolghadr, a heavyweight security figure with a strong military background, is seen as someone suited to handle Iran's current war footing and complex security challenges amid external pressure and internal unrest.

US talking to itself, says Iran as Trump claims wheels of diplomacy turning | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T08:09:40Z

An Iranian military spokesperson mocked US claims of diplomacy, stating the US is 'negotiating with itself' to save face after failing in the war. Reports indicate the US has sent a 15-point ceasefire proposal to Iran, but Al Jazeera's correspondent in Tehran describes 'total confusion' over the status of any negotiations.

Russia, Ukraine tit-for-tat attacks knock out power for over half a million | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T08:21:08Z

Reciprocal attacks on energy infrastructure have left around 450,000 people without power in Russia's Belgorod region and 150,000 in Ukraine's Chernihiv region. The attacks have caused disruptions to heating and water supplies, with repair work expected to take days. Separate incidents include a Ukrainian drone attack sparking a fire at a Russian oil export hub and a Russian attack killing one person in Ukraine's Odesa region.

Dreams of survival: How war has restructured Gaza’s job market | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T08:34:07Z

The war has devastated Gaza's traditional economy, forcing professionals like artist Abdulrahman al-Awadi to abandon their careers for makeshift jobs like running a mobile phone charging station. Widespread destruction and the collapse of basic services have created a 'survival economy' where skilled graduates must adapt to small-scale work to secure a bare-minimum income. This shift illustrates how the conflict has erased traditional professions and created new ones shaped by war and shortages.

Read original article

Is Iran’s negotiating position stronger than when US-Israeli war started? | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T08:38:42Z

Analysts suggest Iran's negotiating position may have hardened since the war began, with Tehran seeking to end the conflict on its own terms and extract major concessions. Iran aims for a post-war order that restores deterrence and provides long-term economic and security guarantees, potentially including fees for Strait of Hormuz passage. Despite US claims of degraded Iranian capabilities, Iran has demonstrated it can still launch precise attacks and maintains significant leverage through its control of the strategic waterway.

Belarus’s Lukashenko makes first visit to North Korea in bid to deepen ties | Russia-Ukraine war News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T09:23:36Z

Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko has begun his first official visit to North Korea to strengthen ties, with plans to sign a treaty of friendship and several agreements. The two countries are united by their support for Russia's war in Ukraine and face Western sanctions. The visit follows increased cooperation, including North Korea sending soldiers and weapons to Russia in exchange for aid, which has reduced Pyongyang's dependence on China.

‘A heinous crime’: Air strikes kill seven fighters in Iraq’s Anbar | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T09:34:59Z

Aerial attacks on a military base in Iraq's Anbar province killed seven fighters and wounded 13, with Iraq's Defence Ministry condemning it as a 'heinous crime'. An Iraqi police source told Al Jazeera the strikes targeted positions of the Iran-aligned Popular Mobilisation Forces (PMF), marking an escalation as Iraq is increasingly drawn into the US-Israeli war on Iran. Iraq has denounced the attack and reserves the right to respond, following a deadly strike on the same base the previous day.

Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz is an international crisis | US-Israel war on Iran | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T10:09:15Z

The disruption of maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz for over two weeks has created a global crisis, as the strait is a critical chokepoint for about 20% of the world's petroleum and a significant portion of LNG. Major energy producers like Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the UAE have had to cut production, invoke force majeure, or reroute exports, causing oil prices to soar to nearly $120 per barrel. The article argues this situation requires global action due to its severe impact on energy markets and potential violation of international law.

Iran says ‘non-hostile’ ships can pass safely through Strait of Hormuz | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T03:31:09Z

Iran has stated that 'non-hostile' ships may transit the Strait of Hormuz safely if they do not participate in or support acts of aggression against Iran and comply with safety regulations. This comes amid a collapse in maritime traffic through the key waterway, which has triggered a global energy crisis. US jury orders Meta to pay $375m for endangering children | Technology News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T04:10:03Z

A jury in New Mexico has ordered Meta to pay $375 million for harming children's mental health and making them vulnerable to sexual exploitation. The verdict marks the first successful US state lawsuit against Meta over child safety issues, with jurors finding the company violated state law by prioritizing profits over safety. Meta says it disagrees with the verdict and will appeal, while a second phase of proceedings to determine additional penalties and platform changes is scheduled for May.

Iran has threatened to boycott its group matches on US soil because of the US-Israel war on Iran. While New Zealand is preparing as if the scheduled match in Los Angeles will proceed, players stated they could accommodate a venue switch to locations like Mexico or Canada.

OpenAI pulls AI video app Sora as concerns grow on deepfake videos | Social Media News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T04:59:56Z

OpenAI is shutting down its social media app Sora, which allowed users to share short-form AI-generated videos but raised concerns about deepfakes and nonconsensual content. The company announced the decision abruptly, blindsiding partners like Disney, with whom it had a $1 billion deal. This move represents OpenAI's first major step to refocus its business on potentially more lucrative areas like coding tools and corporate customers.

From Pakistan to Egypt, Iran war drives up fuel prices in the Global South | Business and Economy News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T05:20:18Z

Developing economies in Asia, Africa, and the Middle East are bearing the brunt of surging energy costs due to the US-Israel war on Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Countries like Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka, which are heavily dependent on imported energy, are implementing measures such as fuel rationing, public holidays, and work-from-home orders to conserve dwindling reserves. Governments are attempting to cushion the public from price shocks with subsidies, but analysts warn the situation could worsen if the war continues.

Amid US-Israeli attacks, people in Iran struggle to survive ailing economy | US-Israel war on Iran | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T05:41:14Z

Iranians are grappling with the economic fallout and a prolonged internet shutdown amid the US-Israeli war on their country. Business activity has significantly declined, with vendors reporting sales at about one-third of usual levels during the Persian New Year period. The population faces high inflation, reduced purchasing power, and job scarcity, exacerbated by years of sanctions and mismanagement, with some families temporarily displaced from major cities.

NASA to spend $20bn on moon base, nuclear-powered Mars spacecraft | Science and Technology News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-24T20:58:04Z

NASA has announced a major overhaul of its Artemis program, scrapping plans for a lunar-orbit space station and committing $20bn over seven years to build a surface base on the moon. The agency also plans to launch a nuclear-powered spacecraft called Space Reactor 1 Freedom by 2028 to demonstrate propulsion technology for Mars missions. These changes reshape contracts and partnerships, shifting focus from the Lunar Gateway to surface infrastructure as the US faces competition from China's lunar ambitions.

The attacks include strikes on the Popular Mobilisation Forces in Anbar province and the Kurdistan Regional Guard in Erbil. Iraq's National Security Council also stated it will lodge a formal complaint with the UN Security Council concerning these acts of aggression.

Unpaid Transportation Security Administration (TSA) workers, a result of a partial government shutdown, have led to mass resignations, staffing shortages, and hours-long security lines at major US airports. The Trump administration has deployed ICE agents to fill gaps, a move criticized due to their lack of training. Analysts warn the situation, compounded by travel complications from the war on Iran, creates an image of systemic dysfunction and questions the safety of US air travel.

US President Donald Trump maintains that negotiations to end the war on Iran are underway, claiming Tehran wants a deal and alluding to a 'present' related to oil and gas. Russian-supplied airpower, intelligence, and battlefield tactics from the Ukraine war are helping Myanmar's military government gain an edge in its civil war. Analysts note the use of Russian-made jets and drones, as well as the adoption of Russian 'meat assault' tactics using waves of conscripted infantry. The 2021 coup and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine have brought the two sanctioned countries closer, with Myanmar providing military assistance to Russia and receiving weapons and support in return.

Philippine president declares energy emergency as impact of Iran war felt | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-25T01:46:20Z

Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. has declared a one-year national energy emergency, citing the 'imminent danger' to the country's energy supply from the US-Israel war on Iran. The declaration empowers the government to procure fuel, combat hoarding, and ensure the supply of essential goods. Transport unions have criticized the move as a 'superficial band-aid,' and the government is reportedly seeking US exemptions to potentially buy oil from sanctioned countries like Iran and Venezuela.

Israel launched new attacks on Tehran, while Iran continued retaliatory strikes, injuring nearly 100 people near an Israeli nuclear facility. The article also notes Saudi Arabia intercepted Iranian drones and expelled Iranian diplomatic staff.

Slovenia’s governing liberals face right-wing populists in tight election 2026-03-22T08:53:45Z

Slovenians are voting in a tight parliamentary election between incumbent liberal Prime Minister Robert Golob and right-wing populist Janez Jansa. Neither candidate is likely to win a parliamentary majority, making smaller coalition partners decisive. A victory for Jansa, an admirer of Donald Trump, could shift Slovenia's foreign policy, reversing moves like recognizing Palestine and imposing an arms embargo on Israel.

Read original article

Why the world should worry about Israel’s nuclear doctrine 2026-03-22T09:01:21Z

This opinion piece argues that Israel's nuclear doctrine, based on 'nuclear opacity,' sets a dangerously low threshold for the use of nuclear weapons. It states that Israeli strategic thinking, shaped by a fear of existential threat, could consider nuclear use against a non-nuclear state if national survival is believed to be at risk. The author contends this risk is heightened by Israel's current multi-front conflicts.

Read original article

Italians vote in high-stakes justice referendum in key test for Meloni 2026-03-22T10:01:22Z

Italians are voting in a two-day referendum on contested judicial reforms proposed by Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's government. Meloni says the reforms will make the judiciary more independent, but critics argue they are a political power grab that weakens judicial independence. The vote is seen as a key political test for Meloni ahead of parliamentary elections next year.

Casualties as Israeli settlers set fire to homes and cars in West Bank | Occupied West Bank News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-22T04:58:22Z

Israeli settlers set fire to homes and vehicles in at least two areas of the occupied West Bank near Jenin, wounding at least one person. The attacks, which occurred during Eid al-Fitr celebrations, also included incidents in Masafer Yatta and other villages involving assaults and stone-throwing. Settler violence in the West Bank has intensified since the war in Gaza began, with over 1,000 Palestinians killed in the territory according to UN figures.

Russia's Movsar Evloev defeated the previously unbeaten Lerone Murphy by majority decision at UFC Fight Night 270 in London. Despite a point deduction for a foul, Evloev's performance, including a strong grappling attack in the final round, secured the win. The victory is expected to earn him a title fight against featherweight champion Alexander Volkanovski.

WHO says attack on Sudan hospital killed 64, including 13 children | Sudan war News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-21T22:43:25Z

The attack damaged key departments, rendering the facility non-functional. This incident brings the total number of fatalities linked to attacks on health facilities during Sudan's war to over 2,000.

The Saudi Foreign Ministry warned that continued attacks would have significant consequences for bilateral relations.

His death was marked by an acrimonious post from former President Donald Trump, while Democratic figures remembered him as a public servant. Mueller's tenure included reforming the FBI after 9/11 but also overseeing controversial surveillance practices.

Flash flooding swamps Hawaii, prompting evacuation orders for 5,500 people | Weather News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-21T18:01:23Z

Heavy rains have caused the worst flooding in Hawaii in 20 years, prompting evacuation orders for about 5,500 people on Oahu's north shore. Officials warned of a high risk of road failure and expressed particular concern about the potential failure of the 120-year-old Wahiawa dam. No deaths have been reported, but at least 200 people have been rescued and 10 hospitalized with hypothermia.

EU urges members to start storing winter gas as Iran war causes price surge | Oil and Gas News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-21T19:24:11Z

The European Union has urged member states to start early on filling gas storage for next winter after attacks on energy facilities in the Gulf caused prices to surge. Energy Commissioner Dan Jorgensen suggested lowering the storage target to 80% and starting early to mitigate price pressure. The call follows an Iranian attack on Qatar's LNG facility, which has reduced global supply and increased competition, though the EU's direct supply from Qatar is limited.

Lin Yu-ting cleared to return to boxing after sex eligibility review | Boxing News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-21T10:40:59Z

Olympic gold medal-winning boxer Lin Yu-ting of Taiwan has been cleared to return to competition at the Asian Boxing Championships following a review of her sex eligibility by World Boxing. The sport's governing body implemented a policy requiring a genetic test, and Lin's national association stated that independent experts confirmed she has been female since birth. The Algerian boxer Imane Khelif, who was also scrutinized at the 2024 Paris Olympics, has not competed under the new policy but has indicated a desire to return.

The site, a key nuclear facility, was also targeted in a previous conflict in June 2025.

Maika Hamano scored the decisive long-range goal in the 17th minute in front of a record-breaking crowd of 74,357 fans. The tournament, which also served as World Cup qualifying, saw Japan dominate, scoring 29 goals and conceding just one across six games.

US says it struck alleged drug trafficking vessel in Pacific, killing two | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-20T20:30:08Z

The US military announced it attacked an alleged drug trafficking vessel in the eastern Pacific, killing two people and recovering one survivor. This strike is part of Operation Southern Spear, a campaign that has killed at least 159 people in 46 incidents since September 2025. The Trump administration argues the strikes deter drug trafficking, but international legal scholars have denounced them as extrajudicial killings.

Ex-minister Gamboa targeted in Costa Rica’s first extradition to the US | Crime News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-20T20:39:59Z

Costa Rica has extradited citizens to the US for the first time in recent history, including former Supreme Court justice Celso Gamboa, who is accused of using government connections to traffic cocaine. This follows a 2025 constitutional amendment that allowed extradition for drug-trafficking and terrorism suspects, with certain conditions. The extradition is seen as part of a broader regional trend of aggressive tactics to combat organized crime in Latin America.

US judge sides with New York Times against Pentagon journalism policies | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-20T23:07:20Z

A US federal judge blocked the Trump administration from enforcing a Pentagon policy that limited news reporters' access, siding with The New York Times which argued the rules were unlawful. Judge Paul Friedman ruled the policy violates journalists' constitutional rights to free speech and due process by restricting press credentials. The ruling is described as a rejection of efforts to impede freedom of the press and reporting on government actions.

Why Pakistani farmers are suing two German companies for deadly 2022 floods | Climate Crisis | Al Jazeera 2026-03-20T04:22:09Z

Thirty-nine Pakistani farmers from Sindh province are suing two German companies, RWE and Heidelberg Materials, alleging their greenhouse gas emissions contributed to the catastrophic 2022 floods. The floods, described as a climate-induced disaster, killed over 1,700 people and caused an estimated $40bn in damages. The farmers' case relates to the companies being among major industrial producers of historical carbon emissions.

US states file lawsuit challenging Trump’s revocation of climate finding | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera 2026-03-19T21:07:29Z

A coalition of 23 US states, led by California and New York, has filed a legal petition challenging the Trump administration's revocation of the 'endangerment finding,' a key scientific determination that climate change threatens human health and the environment. The lawsuit, filed in the US Court of Appeals, also challenges the repeal of tailpipe emissions standards for certain vehicle model years. The states argue the revocation unlawfully removes foundational protections for addressing climate change.

In a separate investigation, FIFA announced no action would be taken against the IFA over allegations concerning Israeli clubs based in the occupied West Bank.

FIFA also set a budget target of $14 billion in revenue for the 2027-30 cycle, which includes future World Cups and development allocations.

The move strains the close ties between the two nations, which are built on shared religion, trade, and cultural exchanges.

The raid is part of broader regional efforts against cartels, though experts warn such 'decapitation strategies' can increase long-term violence.
